"""
=============================================================================
FAMILY-STYLE LAGRANGIAN WORKBENCH
=============================================================================
Goal:
  1) Show the work clearly
  2) Compare the LLC against standard benchmark Lagrangians
  3) Throw a much larger test battery at the model
  4) Sweep many parameter settings instead of relying on one hand-picked point

Models included:
  - Harmonic Oscillator (benchmark)
  - Coupled Oscillator Pair (benchmark)
  - LLC-style 10D coherence-weighted model

Main outputs:
  - Step-by-step Euler-Lagrange structure
  - Shared benchmark tests across models
  - Large parameter sweep for the LLC
  - Plain-English console report
  - CSV summary file

Usage in Colab:
  !pip install jax[cpu] sympy pandas -q
  %run family_lagrangian_workbench.py
=============================================================================
"""

import math
import time
import json
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict
from typing import Callable, Dict, List, Tuple

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import sympy as sp

import jax
import jax.numpy as jnp
from jax import grad, jacfwd, random

jax.config.update("jax_enable_x64", True)

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Pretty printing helpers
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------

def banner(title: str, width: int = 78):
    print("\n" + "=" * width)
    print(title)
    print("=" * width)

def sub(title: str, width: int = 78):
    print("\n" + "-" * width)
    print(title)
    print("-" * width)

def yesno(v: bool) -> str:
    return "YES" if v else "NO"

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Symbolic "show the work"
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------

def symbolic_show_the_work():
    banner("PART 1 вАФ SHOW THE WORK (SYMBOLIC EULER-LAGRANGE STRUCTURE)")

    n = 3  # small symbolic template so the structure is readable
    q = sp.Matrix(sp.symbols('q0:%d' % n, real=True))
    qd = sp.Matrix(sp.symbols('qd0:%d' % n, real=True))

    K = sp.MatrixSymbol('K', n, n)
    chi = sp.Function('chi')(*q)
    V = sp.Function('V')(*q)

    kinetic = sp.Rational(1, 2) * chi * (qd.T * sp.Matrix(K) * qd)[0]
    L = kinetic - V

    print("Template Lagrangian:")
    print("  L(q, qdot) = 1/2 * chi(q) * qdot^T K qdot - V(q)")
    print("\nVector result:")
    print("  d/dt(вИВL/вИВqdot) - вИВL/вИВq = 0")
    print("\nExpanded structure:")
    print("  chi K qddot + chi_dot K qdot - 1/2 (вИЗchi) (qdot^T K qdot) + вИЗV = 0")
    print("\nWhat that means in plain English:")
    print("  1) K sets the base coupling geometry")
    print("  2) chi(q) rescales the kinetic sector")
    print("  3) вИЗchi creates extra nonlinear forcing terms")
    print("  4) V(q) supplies the restoring / driving structure")
    print("\nThat is the general work-shown form the numeric models below are testing.")

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Common model interface
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------

@dataclass
class ModelSpec:
    name: str
    dim: int
    lagrangian: Callable
    q0: jnp.ndarray
    qd0: jnp.ndarray
    parameter_note: str
    open_system: bool

@dataclass
class TestResult:
    model: str
    test: str
    passed: bool
    value: float
    note: str

def mass_matrix(L, q, qd, t=0.0):
    L_func = lambda qq, vv: L(qq, vv, t)
    return jacfwd(grad(L_func, argnums=1), argnums=1)(q, qd)

def grad_q(L, q, qd, t=0.0):
    L_func = lambda qq, vv: L(qq, vv, t)
    return grad(L_func, argnums=0)(q, qd)

def grad_qd(L, q, qd, t=0.0):
    L_func = lambda qq, vv: L(qq, vv, t)
    return grad(L_func, argnums=1)(q, qd)

def hessian_of_scalar(f, q):
    return jacfwd(jacfwd(f))(q)

def energy(L, q, qd, t=0.0):
    p = grad_qd(L, q, qd, t)
    return float(jnp.dot(p, qd) - L(q, qd, t))

def eom(L, q, qd, t=0.0):
    L_func = lambda qq, vv: L(qq, vv, t)
    dLdq = grad(L_func, argnums=0)(q, qd)
    mm = jacfwd(grad(L_func, argnums=1), argnums=1)(q, qd)
    mx = jacfwd(grad(L_func, argnums=1), argnums=0)(q, qd)
    rhs = dLdq - mx @ qd
    return jnp.linalg.lstsq(mm, rhs, rcond=1e-10)[0]

def rk4_step(L, state, t, dt):
    n = state.shape[0] // 2

    def f(s, tt):
        q = s[:n]
        qd = s[n:]
        a = eom(L, q, qd, tt)
        return jnp.concatenate([qd, a])

    k1 = f(state, t)
    k2 = f(state + 0.5 * dt * k1, t + 0.5 * dt)
    k3 = f(state + 0.5 * dt * k2, t + 0.5 * dt)
    k4 = f(state + dt * k3, t + dt)
    return state + (dt / 6.0) * (k1 + 2*k2 + 2*k3 + 2*k3 + k4)

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Benchmark model 1 вАФ Harmonic oscillator
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------

def make_harmonic():
    k = 1.5
    m = 2.0

    def L(q, qd, t=0.0):
        return 0.5 * m * jnp.dot(qd, qd) - 0.5 * k * jnp.dot(q, q)

    return ModelSpec(
        name="Harmonic Oscillator",
        dim=1,
        lagrangian=L,
        q0=jnp.array([1.0]),
        qd0=jnp.array([0.2]),
        parameter_note="m=2.0, k=1.5",
        open_system=False,
    )

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Benchmark model 2 вАФ Coupled oscillator pair
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------

def make_coupled_pair():
    M = jnp.array([[2.0, 0.0], [0.0, 1.5]])
    Kspring = jnp.array([[3.0, -0.8], [-0.8, 2.5]])

    def L(q, qd, t=0.0):
        kin = 0.5 * (qd @ M @ qd)
        pot = 0.5 * (q @ Kspring @ q)
        return kin - pot

    return ModelSpec(
        name="Coupled Oscillator Pair",
        dim=2,
        lagrangian=L,
        q0=jnp.array([0.8, -0.4]),
        qd0=jnp.array([0.1, 0.3]),
        parameter_note="Mass and spring matrices fixed",
        open_system=False,
    )

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# LLC-style model
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------

VAR_SHORT = ['G', 'M', 'E', 'S', 'T', 'K', 'R', 'Q', 'F', 'C']
N = 10
iG, iM, iE, iS, iT, iK, iR, iQ, iF, iC = range(10)
BASE_PAIRS = [(iG, iQ), (iM, iF), (iE, iC), (iS, iR), (iT, iK)]

def build_pair_matrix(weights, pair_strength, pairs=BASE_PAIRS):
    M = jnp.diag(weights)
    for i, j in pairs:
        M = M.at[i, j].set(pair_strength)
        M = M.at[j, i].set(pair_strength)
    return M

def make_llc(weights=None, pair_strength=0.45, confine=0.01, params=None, time_open=True):
    if weights is None:
        weights = jnp.array([1.0, 0.8, 1.2, 1.5, 0.7, 0.6, 0.9, 1.1, 0.5, 1.3], dtype=jnp.float64)
    if params is None:
        params = {'G0': 1.0, 'R_J': 1.0, 'E0': 0.5, 'k_ent': 0.1, 'S0': 0.5, 'lam': 0.2, 'n_f': 3}

    KIN = build_pair_matrix(weights, pair_strength)

    def chi(q, t=0.0):
        G, M, E, S, T, K, R, Q, F, C = q

        Ss = jnp.clip(jnp.abs(S), 1e-6, 1e6)
        Rs = jnp.clip(R, -10, 10)
        g_exp = jnp.clip(Rs / Ss, -20, 20)
        g_num = params['G0'] * jnp.exp(g_exp) * params['R_J'] * jnp.abs(G)

        tt = (T * t) if time_open else 0.0
        te = jnp.clip(tt, -20, 20)
        ent = params['E0'] * jnp.abs(E) * jnp.exp(params['k_ent'] * te)
        sin_d = params['S0'] * jnp.exp(-params['lam'] * jnp.abs(Rs) * jnp.clip(te, 0, 20))
        t1 = g_num / (1 + ent + sin_d)

        t2 = jnp.exp(-jnp.clip(jnp.abs(Q) * jnp.abs(C), 0, 20))

        Ks = jnp.clip(jnp.abs(K), 0.1, 10)
        Fs = jnp.clip(jnp.abs(F), 0, 10)
        fs = sum((Fs / params['n_f']) * jnp.exp(-i / Ks) for i in range(1, params['n_f'] + 1))
        t3 = 1 + fs

        Ms = jnp.clip(jnp.abs(M), 1e-6, 1e6)
        t4 = 1 + Ms * Ss / (1 + Ms**2 + Ss**2)

        return t1 * t2 * t3 * t4

    def L(q, qd, t=0.0):
        c = jnp.clip(jnp.abs(chi(q, t)), 1e-30, 1e10)
        kin = 0.5 * c * (qd @ KIN @ qd)
        pot = q[iS] * chi(q, t) + confine * jnp.sum(1.0 / jnp.clip(jnp.abs(q), 1e-6, 1e6) ** 2)
        return kin - pot

    spec = ModelSpec(
        name="LLC-style 10D Model" + (" (open)" if time_open else " (frozen-time)"),
        dim=10,
        lagrangian=L,
        q0=jnp.array([1.0, 0.8, 1.2, 1.5, 0.7, 0.6, 0.9, 1.1, 0.5, 1.3], dtype=jnp.float64),
        qd0=0.1 * jnp.array([0.1, -0.05, 0.08, -0.03, 0.02, 0.06, -0.04, 0.07, -0.02, 0.05], dtype=jnp.float64),
        parameter_note=f"pair_strength={pair_strength}, confine={confine}, params={params}",
        open_system=time_open,
    )
    spec.chi = chi
    spec.KIN = KIN
    spec.params = params
    spec.pair_strength = pair_strength
    spec.confine = confine
    return spec

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Shared tests
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------

def test_local_well_posedness(spec: ModelSpec) -> List[TestResult]:
    q, qd = spec.q0, spec.qd0
    mm = mass_matrix(spec.lagrangian, q, qd)
    eigs = np.array(jnp.linalg.eigvalsh(mm))
    rank = int(np.sum(np.abs(eigs) > 1e-10))
    cond = float(jnp.linalg.cond(mm))

    return [
        TestResult(spec.name, "Mass matrix full rank", rank == spec.dim, float(rank), f"rank={rank}/{spec.dim}"),
        TestResult(spec.name, "Mass matrix positive eigenvalues", bool(np.all(eigs > 0)), float(np.min(eigs)), f"min_eig={np.min(eigs):.6g}"),
        TestResult(spec.name, "Mass matrix condition reasonable", cond < 1e6, cond, f"cond={cond:.3e}"),
    ]

def test_short_trajectory(spec: ModelSpec, steps=250, dt=0.004) -> List[TestResult]:
    state = jnp.concatenate([spec.q0, spec.qd0])
    t = 0.0
    energies = []
    max_abs_q = []
    ok = True
    reason = "bounded"
    try:
        for _ in range(steps):
            state = rk4_step(spec.lagrangian, state, t, dt)
            t += dt
            q = state[:spec.dim]
            qd = state[spec.dim:]
            energies.append(energy(spec.lagrangian, q, qd, t))
            max_abs_q.append(float(jnp.max(jnp.abs(q))))
            if not np.isfinite(np.array(q)).all() or not np.isfinite(np.array(qd)).all():
                ok = False
                reason = "NaN or inf encountered"
                break
    except Exception as e:
        ok = False
        reason = f"exception: {e}"

    results = [
        TestResult(spec.name, "Short trajectory remains finite", ok, float(max(max_abs_q) if max_abs_q else np.nan), reason),
    ]

    if ok and len(energies) >= 2:
        drift = abs(energies[-1] - energies[0]) / (abs(energies[0]) + 1e-12)
        note = "open system drift expected" if spec.open_system else "closed system drift should stay small"
        results.append(TestResult(spec.name, "Energy drift small / interpretable", drift < (0.6 if spec.open_system else 0.08), float(drift), note))
    return results

def test_finite_gradients(spec: ModelSpec) -> List[TestResult]:
    gq = np.array(grad_q(spec.lagrangian, spec.q0, spec.qd0))
    gqd = np.array(grad_qd(spec.lagrangian, spec.q0, spec.qd0))
    return [
        TestResult(spec.name, "Finite вИВL/вИВq", bool(np.isfinite(gq).all()), float(np.linalg.norm(gq)), "gradient in q-space"),
        TestResult(spec.name, "Finite вИВL/вИВqdot", bool(np.isfinite(gqd).all()), float(np.linalg.norm(gqd)), "gradient in velocity-space"),
    ]

def test_llc_specific(spec: ModelSpec) -> List[TestResult]:
    if "LLC-style" not in spec.name:
        return []

    H = hessian_of_scalar(lambda qq: spec.chi(qq, 0.0), spec.q0)
    off = H - jnp.diag(jnp.diag(H))
    ratio = float(jnp.linalg.norm(off) / (jnp.linalg.norm(jnp.diag(H)) + 1e-30))

    q_small_vals = []
    for i in range(spec.dim):
        q_test = spec.q0.at[i].set(1e-3)
        q_small_vals.append(float(spec.chi(q_test, 0.0)))
    baseline = float(spec.chi(spec.q0, 0.0))
    suppression_ratio = max(q_small_vals) / (baseline + 1e-12)

    pair_vals = []
    nonpair_vals = []
    pair_set = set(BASE_PAIRS) | set((b, a) for a, b in BASE_PAIRS)
    Hnp = np.abs(np.array(H))
    for i in range(spec.dim):
        for j in range(i + 1, spec.dim):
            if (i, j) in pair_set:
                pair_vals.append(Hnp[i, j])
            else:
                nonpair_vals.append(Hnp[i, j])

    pair_adv = float((np.mean(pair_vals) + 1e-12) / (np.mean(nonpair_vals) + 1e-12))

    return [
        TestResult(spec.name, "Local coupling ratio > 1%", ratio > 0.01, ratio, "local non-separability indicator only"),
        TestResult(spec.name, "Zeroing variables suppresses chi", suppression_ratio < 0.5, suppression_ratio, "sensitivity test, not strict product proof"),
        TestResult(spec.name, "Pair couplings stronger on average", pair_adv > 1.0, pair_adv, "pair structure partly reflects imposed K"),
    ]

def run_shared_battery(models: List[ModelSpec]) -> List[TestResult]:
    banner("PART 2 вАФ SHARED BATTERY ACROSS DIFFERENT LAGRANGIANS")
    results: List[TestResult] = []
    for spec in models:
        sub(f"Model: {spec.name}")
        print(f"Dimension: {spec.dim}")
        print(f"Notes: {spec.parameter_note}")

        these = []
        these.extend(test_local_well_posedness(spec))
        these.extend(test_finite_gradients(spec))
        these.extend(test_short_trajectory(spec))
        these.extend(test_llc_specific(spec))
        results.extend(these)

        for r in these:
            status = "PASS" if r.passed else "FAIL"
            print(f"{status:>4} | {r.test:<38} | value={r.value:.6g} | {r.note}")
    return results

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Large LLC stress testing
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------

def random_llc_spec(key):
    # Randomize weights, pair strength, confinement, and chi parameters
    key, k1, k2, k3, k4, k5, k6, k7 = random.split(key, 8)

    weights = random.uniform(k1, (10,), minval=0.35, maxval=2.2)
    pair_strength = float(random.uniform(k2, (), minval=0.05, maxval=0.9))
    confine = float(random.uniform(k3, (), minval=0.0, maxval=0.05))

    params = {
        'G0': float(random.uniform(k4, (), minval=0.5, maxval=2.0)),
        'R_J': float(random.uniform(k5, (), minval=0.5, maxval=2.0)),
        'E0': float(random.uniform(k6, (), minval=0.1, maxval=1.5)),
        'k_ent': float(random.uniform(k7, (), minval=0.0, maxval=0.35)),
        'S0': float(0.2 + 0.8 * np.random.rand()),
        'lam': float(0.05 + 0.5 * np.random.rand()),
        'n_f': int(np.random.choice([2, 3, 4, 5])),
    }

    time_open = bool(np.random.choice([True, False]))
    return key, make_llc(weights=weights, pair_strength=pair_strength, confine=confine, params=params, time_open=time_open)

def run_llc_stress_sweep(num_samples=60, seed=2828) -> Tuple[pd.DataFrame, List[TestResult]]:
    banner("PART 3 вАФ LLC STRESS SWEEP (MANY PARAMETERS, NOT ONE POINT)")
    print(f"Running {num_samples} randomized LLC builds...")

    key = random.PRNGKey(seed)
    rows = []
    all_results = []

    t0 = time.time()
    for idx in range(num_samples):
        key, spec = random_llc_spec(key)

        res = []
        res.extend(test_local_well_posedness(spec))
        res.extend(test_finite_gradients(spec))
        res.extend(test_short_trajectory(spec, steps=180, dt=0.004))
        res.extend(test_llc_specific(spec))

        all_results.extend(res)

        pass_rate = np.mean([1.0 if r.passed else 0.0 for r in res])
        min_rank = min([r.value for r in res if r.test == "Mass matrix full rank"], default=np.nan)
        rows.append({
            "sample_id": idx,
            "model": spec.name,
            "pair_strength": spec.pair_strength,
            "confine": spec.confine,
            "open_system": spec.open_system,
            "test_count": len(res),
            "pass_rate": pass_rate,
            "all_pass": int(all(r.passed for r in res)),
        })

    elapsed = time.time() - t0
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows)

    print(f"Done in {elapsed:.2f}s")
    print(f"Mean pass rate: {df['pass_rate'].mean():.3f}")
    print(f"All-pass runs: {int(df['all_pass'].sum())}/{len(df)}")
    print(f"Closed runs: {int((~df['open_system']).sum())} | Open runs: {int(df['open_system'].sum())}")

    return df, all_results

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Comparison summary table
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------

def summarize_results(results: List[TestResult]) -> pd.DataFrame:
    rows = []
    for r in results:
        rows.append(asdict(r))
    df = pd.DataFrame(rows)
    return df

def model_summary_table(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.DataFrame:
    out = (
        df.groupby("model")
        .agg(
            tests=("passed", "size"),
            passes=("passed", "sum"),
            mean_value=("value", "mean"),
        )
        .reset_index()
    )
    out["pass_rate"] = out["passes"] / out["tests"]
    return out.sort_values(["pass_rate", "passes"], ascending=[False, False])

# -------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Main
# -------------------------------------------------------------------------

def main():
    symbolic_show_the_work()

    models = [
        make_harmonic(),
        make_coupled_pair(),
        make_llc(time_open=False),
        make_llc(time_open=True),
    ]

    shared_results = run_shared_battery(models)
    shared_df = summarize_results(shared_results)
    shared_summary = model_summary_table(shared_df)

    banner("PART 4 вАФ SIDE-BY-SIDE COMPARISON")
    print(shared_summary.to_string(index=False))

    stress_df, stress_results = run_llc_stress_sweep(num_samples=60, seed=2828)
    stress_result_df = summarize_results(stress_results)

    banner("PART 5 вАФ PLAIN-ENGLISH TAKEAWAYS")
    print("1) The work is shown explicitly: the Euler-Lagrange structure is written out first.")
    print("2) The LLC is not being tested in isolation; it is benchmarked beside standard models.")
    print("3) The LLC is being stress-tested over many parameter settings, not just one favorite point.")
    print("4) Local strong coupling can be supported numerically, but global irreducibility is still a stronger claim.")
    print("5) Pair-strength effects can be measured, but those effects should be interpreted in light of the imposed K matrix.")
    print("6) The stronger the claim, the more it should be tied to a test that directly measures it.")

    # Save outputs
    shared_df.to_csv("family_lagrangian_shared_results.csv", index=False)
    shared_summary.to_csv("family_lagrangian_model_summary.csv", index=False)
    stress_df.to_csv("family_lagrangian_stress_runs.csv", index=False)
    stress_result_df.to_csv("family_lagrangian_stress_results.csv", index=False)

    metadata = {

Here are the two ways to check and manage TTS voices in Windows вАФ one via modern Settings and one via the classic Control Panel.

Path 1 вАФ Windows Settings (Modern, Recommended)
This is where you install new voices for apps like POF 2828 to detect:

Press Win + I to open Settings

Go to Time & Language вЖТ Speech

Scroll down to Manage voices

Click Add voices to install more (Microsoft Jenny, Aria, Guy, etc.)

Already-installed voices appear in the list here

Path 2 вАФ Control Panel (Legacy SAPI5 Voices)
This controls the default voice used by older apps like POF 2828's synthesizer:

Press Win + R, type control вЖТ hit Enter

Go to Ease of Access вЖТ Speech Recognition

Click Text to Speech in the left panel

Use the Voice selection dropdown to change the active default voice

Click Preview Voice to test it, then Apply

You can also open it directly by pressing Win + R and running:

text
C:\Windows\System32\Speech\SpeechUX\sapi.cpl
Quick PowerShell Check
To instantly see every voice Windows currently has registered, run this in PowerShell:

powershell
Add-Type -AssemblyName System.Speech
(New-Object System.Speech.Synthesis.SpeechSynthesizer).GetInstalledVoices() |
  Select -ExpandProperty VoiceInfo |
  Select Name, Culture, Gender, Age
This will reveal exactly which voices POF 2828 can see (SAPI5 registry) vs. what Windows Settings shows вАФ if they differ, the OneCore registry merge fix from before is your solution.

There's no single "right" way to structure an Obsidian vault вАФ the community has developed several distinct philosophies, each with a different mental model. Here's a breakdown of the major schools of thought:

Zettelkasten (Bottom-Up, Idea-Centric)
Originally developed by sociologist Niklas Luhmann, this is arguably the most influential PKM philosophy in the Obsidian world. The core idea: notes are atomic (one idea per note), written in your own words, and linked to each other organically. There are no rigid folders вАФ structure emerges from connections. Notes evolve through three stages:

Fleeting notes вАФ quick captures, disposable

Literature notes вАФ source-based summaries

Permanent/Evergreen notes вАФ distilled, linked ideas in your own words

The debate in the community is whether truly atomic notes become too granular вАФ hundreds of two-line notes that are hard to navigate.

PARA (Top-Down, Action-Oriented)
Developed by Tiago Forte (Building a Second Brain), PARA organizes everything by actionability, not topic:

Projects вАФ active goals with deadlines

Areas вАФ ongoing responsibilities (health, finances)

Resources вАФ reference material by topic

Archive вАФ inactive items

PARA is folder-heavy and highly structured. It's criticized for being too rigid for pure knowledge work but excels for project-driven people.

LYT вАФ Linking Your Thinking (Middle-Out, MOC-Based)
Created by Nick Milo, LYT is the most Obsidian-native philosophy. Its key innovation is the Map of Content (MOC) вАФ a special note that acts as a hub linking related notes together, without forcing a rigid folder hierarchy. The vault is organized around an ACE framework:

Atlas вАФ knowledge maps and MOCs

Calendar вАФ daily/periodic notes

Efforts вАФ active projects and work

The Ideaverse starter kit has been downloaded 70,000+ times, making it the most widely adopted structure.

Johnny.Decimal (Numbered Folder System)
A hyper-organized folder approach using a decimal numbering system. Every area of life gets a number range (e.g., 10-19 = Health, 20-29 = Work), with subcategories as decimals (21.01, 21.02). Its strength is findability вАФ you always know exactly where something lives. Its weakness is inflexibility; it doesn't naturally support the emergent linking that makes Obsidian powerful. Many users hybrid it with Zettelkasten.

Brutalist / Minimalist (Radical Simplicity)
A philosophy focused on separation of concerns вАФ notes are not tasks, not projects, not essays. Four principles only: separate functions, constructivist mindset, use prose to think, and make links explicit. No decoration, no CSS polish, no over-engineering. This resonates with people who want Obsidian as a pure thinking tool, not a productivity system.

The Pragmatic Hybrid (Most Common in Practice)
Most experienced Obsidian users land here вАФ combining elements from multiple philosophies:

Philosophy	What they borrow
Zettelkasten	Atomic notes, evergreen notes, emergence
PARA	Top-level folder structure for active work
LYT	MOCs as navigation hubs
Johnny.Decimal	Numbered prefixes for top-level areas
The Obsidian forum consensus is telling: "Talk to ten people and you'll get twelve different ways to organize your vault". The structural philosophy matters far less than starting simply and letting your system evolve вАФ heavy upfront structure often collapses under real use.

Given your research-heavy, AI-integrated workflow, the LYT + Zettelkasten hybrid tends to be the sweet spot вАФ MOCs for navigating large knowledge domains, atomic notes for formal concepts, and minimal folders so your graph view stays meaningful rather than fragmented.

You've hit the exact right problem вАФ 28 MP3 files is a lot to host and manage. Here's the full picture of your options, from easiest to most powerful:

The Real Problem You're Describing
28 audio files √Ч ~5-15MB each = potentially 200-400MB total. You can't email that. You need somewhere to host them so family just clicks a link.

Best Options for Your Situation
Option 1 вАФ Google Drive (Simplest, Free, Forever)
Upload all 28 MP3s to a Google Drive folder, set sharing to "Anyone with the link," and embed them directly in your story page:

xml
<!-- Replace FILE_ID with the ID from your Google Drive share link -->
<audio controls>
  <source src="https://drive.google.com/uc?export=download&id=FILE_ID" type="audio/mpeg">
</audio>
Family gets one link to your page, clicks play вАФ done. No account needed on their end.

Option 2 вАФ Spotify for Creators (Best if You Want a Real Podcast)
Completely free, unlimited episodes, unlimited storage. Upload all 28 episodes, get a shareable link for each. Family can listen in any browser, Spotify app, or Apple Podcasts вАФ no account required to just click and play. This is genuinely the cleanest solution for sharing with family at scale.

Option 3 вАФ RedCircle (Free, Unlimited)
Same as Spotify for Creators but gives you an embeddable player you can drop right into your story HTML page. So the story page has the read-along text AND an embedded audio player вАФ they can read along while listening.

The Smart Architecture for Your Stack
Since your Rust backend already renders audio via Edge TTS, here's the full workflow:

text
Write story in POF 2828
    вЖУ
Rust renders each page вЖТ MP3 (en-US-AriaNeural)
    вЖУ
Upload MP3s to Google Drive or Spotify for Creators
    вЖУ
Embed player in your story HTML page
    вЖУ
Email family one link вЖТ they click play in ANY browser
You pre-render once, they play forever вАФ no Edge requirement, no voices installed, no setup on their end at all.

Great news вАФ those whiteboards on Emergent Mind are generated by a tool called Nano Banana, and there are several ways to replicate that style, including a dark/black version for your vault. Here's the full breakdown:

What Emergent Mind Uses
Those sketchy hand-drawn whiteboard explainers are generated by Nano Banana вАФ a tool specifically built to convert research papers into visual whiteboard-style breakdowns. Emergent Mind pipes arXiv papers through it automatically.

Your Best Options
1. Napkin AI вАФ Closest Match, Free Tier
napkin.ai вАФ paste any text or concept and it auto-generates diagram-style visual explanations. It has a dark/black canvas mode, works with your own research notes, and outputs SVG you can embed anywhere. This is probably the fastest path to what you want for your Obsidian vault.

2. PromptBoard вАФ MCP Whiteboard for AI Agents
github.com/pjueon/promptboard вАФ literally an MCP server that gives AI agents a shared whiteboard canvas. This means Claude or any MCP-connected AI can draw diagrams directly onto a whiteboard as part of a conversation. This fits perfectly into your existing MCP stack.

3. 302 AI Whiteboard вАФ Excalidraw + AI
github.com/302ai/302_whiteboard вАФ Excalidraw core with AI that generates Mermaid diagrams and sketchy-style visuals from prompts. Excalidraw natively has a dark mode with that hand-drawn aesthetic you want.

4. Excalidraw Directly (Best for Black Background)
excalidraw.com вАФ the underlying engine most of these tools use. Set theme to dark, and every diagram gets that black background with white sketchy lines вАФ exactly the aesthetic you're after. You can use Claude or GPT to generate the diagram layout as Mermaid/JSON, then render it in Excalidraw.

Quickest Path to Your Black Whiteboard Style
text
Claude/GPT вЖТ generates concept breakdown as Mermaid or Excalidraw JSON
     вЖУ
Excalidraw (dark mode) вЖТ renders it as black bg + white sketch lines
     вЖУ
Export as PNG/SVG вЖТ embed in Obsidian note or story page
For your knowledge vault specifically, the Obsidian Excalidraw plugin by Zsolt Viczian is the most powerful integration вАФ it lets you draw, embed AI-generated diagrams, and link them to notes directly inside Obsidian. Thousands of people use it for exactly the visual PKM style you're describing.
